breath_support.selector_heading: If the main limiter is BREATH / SUPPORT

exercises.name: The Farinelli Maneuver
exercises.detail:
- Use when: suspended breath control and phrase stamina.
- Pedagogical target: Suspended Breath Control.
- How to do it: Inhale for 4 counts, suspend (hold with open glottis) for 4, exhale for 4; increase to 10+ counts.
- How it should feel: Inhale low and wide, suspend without closing the throat, then release like a slow leak.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop or reduce intensity if dizziness, shoulder lifting, throat gripping, or breath-holding appears.
- Pass/Fail metric: Counts are even and the throat stays open during the suspension.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Rib Cage Stationary Drill
exercises.detail:
- Use when: rib stability and appoggio awareness.
- Pedagogical target: Thoracic Stability.
- How to do it: Place hands on lower ribs; inhale/exhale while keeping the ribs expanded and the sternum high.
- How it should feel: Keep the ribs gently open as if the body is staying wide around the sound.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop or reduce intensity if dizziness, shoulder lifting, throat gripping, or breath-holding appears.
- Pass/Fail metric: Ribs do not collapse at the start of the exhale.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: The Sibilant Hiss
exercises.detail:
- Use when: steady airflow and breath metering.
- Pedagogical target: Steady Airflow Management.
- How to do it: Inhale and release a constant [s] sound for as long as possible, aiming for 30-60 seconds.
- How it should feel: Let the [s] feel like one thin, steady thread of air.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop or reduce intensity if dizziness, shoulder lifting, throat gripping, or breath-holding appears.
- Pass/Fail metric: The hiss stays even with no sudden bursts or fading.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Pulsated Fricatives
exercises.detail:
- Use when: abdominal flexibility and rhythmic breath pulses.
- Pedagogical target: Abdominal Flexibility.
- How to do it: Rapid, rhythmic bursts of [f] or [v] sounds (e.g., in triplets) to engage the abdominal wall.
- How it should feel: Let the belly wall bounce lightly without locking the throat.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop or reduce intensity if dizziness, shoulder lifting, throat gripping, or breath-holding appears.
- Pass/Fail metric: Pulses are even and quick without neck tension.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: The Tissue Flutter
exercises.detail:
- Use when: visual airflow feedback.
- Pedagogical target: Visual Flow Biofeedback.
- How to do it: Sustaining a vowel while blowing against a single tissue; a steady angle indicates consistent air pressure.
- How it should feel: Keep the tissue angle steady; do not blast it.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop or reduce intensity if dizziness, shoulder lifting, throat gripping, or breath-holding appears.
- Pass/Fail metric: Tissue movement stays consistent across the vowel.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Back-Breathing Expansion
exercises.detail:
- Use when: breath control, airflow steadiness, support, posture, phrase stamina.
- Pedagogical target: Posterior Lobe Activation.
- How to do it: Place hands on the lower back/kidney area and breathe until expansion is felt against the thumbs.
- How it should feel: Feel the lower ribs widen and stay buoyant; throat quiet; shoulders heavy; airflow steady rather than dumped.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop or reduce intensity if dizziness, shoulder lifting, throat gripping, or breath-holding appears.
- Pass/Fail metric: Airflow stays even for the target count and the body remains expanded without visible neck effort.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Silent Inhalation Drill
exercises.detail:
- Use when: silent, open-throat inhalation.
- Pedagogical target: Laryngeal Abduction.
- How to do it: Rapidly inhaling through an open throat without making noise to ensure full glottal opening.
- How it should feel: Breathe as if surprised, but silent — cool air, open back of throat.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop or reduce intensity if dizziness, shoulder lifting, throat gripping, or breath-holding appears.
- Pass/Fail metric: No audible gasp or shoulder lift.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Axial Alignment Scan
exercises.detail:
- Use when: breath control, airflow steadiness, support, posture, phrase stamina.
- Pedagogical target: Postural Equilibrium.
- How to do it: Standing with feet shoulder-width, knees unlocked, and weight evenly distributed for optimal airflow.
- How it should feel: Feel the lower ribs widen and stay buoyant; throat quiet; shoulders heavy; airflow steady rather than dumped.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop or reduce intensity if dizziness, shoulder lifting, throat gripping, or breath-holding appears.
- Pass/Fail metric: Airflow stays even for the target count and the body remains expanded without visible neck effort.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

breath_support.next_take_cue: Keep the ribs wide and let the air meter out slowly.

pressed_strained.selector_heading: If the singer sounds PRESSED / STRAINED

exercises.name: Straw Phonation in Air
exercises.detail:
- Use when: easy laryngeal reset and efficient phonation.
- Pedagogical target: Increased Vocal Tract Inertance.
- How to do it: Phonating into a 3mm straw while doing pitch glides to stabilize the larynx.
- How it should feel: Let the straw do the work; small steady sound, not loud.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if back-pressure becomes throat pressure, bubbles are violent, or the singer coughs/feels squeezed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Glide feels smoother after 1–2 minutes.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Straw Phonation in Water
exercises.detail:
- Use when: gentle vocal fold massage and pressure balance.
- Pedagogical target: Water-Resistance Massage.
- How to do it: Phonating into a straw submerged 2-5cm in water; bubbles provide visual/tactile feedback.
- How it should feel: Small even bubbles; water should massage, not fight you.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if back-pressure becomes throat pressure, bubbles are violent, or the singer coughs/feels squeezed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Bubbles are steady and the voice feels easier afterwards.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Lip Trills (Lip Bubbles)
exercises.detail:
- Use when: balancing airflow and closure.
- Pedagogical target: Subglottic/Supraglottic Balance.
- How to do it: Rapidly vibrating the lips while sliding through the range to balance air pressure.
- How it should feel: Keep the lips bubbling freely; if they stop, reduce pressure.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if back-pressure becomes throat pressure, bubbles are violent, or the singer coughs/feels squeezed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Trill continues through the range without forcing.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Puffed Cheeks (Blowfish)
exercises.detail:
- Use when: resetting pressure, reducing strain, warming up, improving efficient closure.
- Pedagogical target: Intrinsic Muscle Reset.
- How to do it: Phonating with puffed cheeks and nearly closed lips to create internal pressure.
- How it should feel: Feel easy back-pressure and forward buzz; the throat should feel assisted, not forced.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if back-pressure becomes throat pressure, bubbles are violent, or the singer coughs/feels squeezed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Sound becomes easier, steadier, and less pressed; bubbles/trills/resonance remain consistent.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Hand-Over-Mouth Seal
exercises.detail:
- Use when: instant pressure reset on difficult song phrases.
- Pedagogical target: Instant Supraglottic Pressure.
- How to do it: Placing the palm over the mouth to create a 90% occlusion while singing a difficult passage.
- How it should feel: Cover the mouth gently and sing the phrase at practice volume.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if back-pressure becomes throat pressure, bubbles are violent, or the singer coughs/feels squeezed.
- Pass/Fail metric: The phrase feels easier when the hand is removed.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: The Chewing Method
exercises.detail:
- Use when: releasing jaw/tongue interference.
- Pedagogical target: Muscular Interference Release.
- How to do it: Singing a phrase while performing a chewing motion to relax the entire tract.
- How it should feel: Sing while gently chewing, like the mouth is too lazy to clamp.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the jaw locks, tongue pulls back, vowels distort aggressively, or the tone becomes swallowed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Tone frees up and text remains understandable.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Post-Performance Yawn-Sigh
exercises.detail:
- Use when: laryngeal neutralising after performance.
- Pedagogical target: Laryngeal Neutralization.
- How to do it: Performing 3-5 relaxed yawn-sighs to reset the larynx to a neutral state.
- How it should feel: Yawn-sigh without drama; let the throat widen and release.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop and recommend rest/medical review if pain, persistent hoarseness, loss of range, or fatigue continues.
- Pass/Fail metric: Neck and throat feel less held after 3–5 reps.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

pressed_strained.next_take_cue: Let the tool create back-pressure so the throat does less work.

breathy_leaky.selector_heading: If the singer sounds BREATHY / LEAKY

exercises.name: Vocal Fry to Modal
exercises.detail:
- Use when: register blending, mix coordination, high-note access, chest/head balance.
- Pedagogical target: Adductory Warm-up.
- How to do it: Starting with a "bubbly" vocal fry and smoothly transitioning into a clear tone.
- How it should feel: Feel the pitch change by thinning, widening, or releasing — not by squeezing the throat or lifting the chin.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the singer pushes volume, jams the jaw, strains high notes, or loses easy speech-like release.
- Pass/Fail metric: Transition feels smoother with less break, less throat pressure, and more stable tone.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Staccato Arpeggios
exercises.detail:
- Use when: register blending, mix coordination, high-note access, chest/head balance.
- Pedagogical target: Rapid Adduction Training.
- How to do it: Singing 1-3-5-8-5-3-1 on short, detached vowels to practice precise glottal closure.
- How it should feel: Feel the pitch change by thinning, widening, or releasing — not by squeezing the throat or lifting the chin.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the singer pushes volume, jams the jaw, strains high notes, or loses easy speech-like release.
- Pass/Fail metric: Transition feels smoother with less break, less throat pressure, and more stable tone.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Bratty "Ney" Scales
exercises.detail:
- Use when: cord closure and mix energy.
- Pedagogical target: Mixed Voice Coordination.
- How to do it: Singing an octave repeat scale on a bright "Ney" to encourage cord closure in the mix.
- How it should feel: Make it bratty and bright, like a cheeky ‘ney’, without yelling.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the singer pushes volume, jams the jaw, strains high notes, or loses easy speech-like release.
- Pass/Fail metric: High notes feel more forward, not heavier.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: The Voiced [v] Glide
exercises.detail:
- Use when: resetting pressure, reducing strain, warming up, improving efficient closure.
- Pedagogical target: Labial Resistance.
- How to do it: Sustaining a [v] or [z] sound while sliding from low to high pitches.
- How it should feel: Feel easy back-pressure and forward buzz; the throat should feel assisted, not forced.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if back-pressure becomes throat pressure, bubbles are violent, or the singer coughs/feels squeezed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Sound becomes easier, steadier, and less pressed; bubbles/trills/resonance remain consistent.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: VFE Sustain [i] (Warm-up)
exercises.detail:
- Use when: gentle glottal efficiency and stamina.
- Pedagogical target: Glottal Efficiency.
- How to do it: Sustain [i] on a specific pitch (F) for as long as possible with forward focus.
- How it should feel: Sustain softly with forward focus; never squeeze for length.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop and recommend rest/medical review if pain, persistent hoarseness, loss of range, or fatigue continues.
- Pass/Fail metric: Duration improves without roughness.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: VFE Power Adductory Sustain
exercises.detail:
- Use when: vocal stamina, gentle strengthening, cool-down, recovery, long-term maintenance.
- Pedagogical target: Glottal Closure Stamina.
- How to do it: Sustain the notes C-D-E-F-G on "ol" (from knoll) for as long as possible.
- How it should feel: Keep it soft, easy, and restorative; the voice should feel clearer after, not more tired.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop and recommend rest/medical review if pain, persistent hoarseness, loss of range, or fatigue continues.
- Pass/Fail metric: Voice feels easier after the drill; no increase in strain, dryness, or fatigue.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

breathy_leaky.next_take_cue: Find clean cord contact without squeezing.

high_notes.selector_heading: If high notes are the issue

exercises.name: The Falsetto Owl "Hoot"
exercises.detail:
- Use when: register blending, mix coordination, high-note access, chest/head balance.
- Pedagogical target: CT Muscle Isolation.
- How to do it: Singing an [u] vowel in a light, breathy falsetto to strengthen the lengthener muscles.
- How it should feel: Feel the pitch change by thinning, widening, or releasing — not by squeezing the throat or lifting the chin.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the singer pushes volume, jams the jaw, strains high notes, or loses easy speech-like release.
- Pass/Fail metric: Transition feels smoother with less break, less throat pressure, and more stable tone.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: The Siren (Octave Glissando)
exercises.detail:
- Use when: smoothing chest-to-head transition.
- Pedagogical target: TA/CT Blending.
- How to do it: Sliding from the lowest chest voice to the highest falsetto on an [u] or [o] vowel.
- How it should feel: Slide like one continuous ribbon, letting the voice lighten before it needs to.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the singer pushes volume, jams the jaw, strains high notes, or loses easy speech-like release.
- Pass/Fail metric: No obvious flip or throat squeeze through the bridge.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: The "Gee" (Dopy sound)
exercises.detail:
- Use when: register blending, mix coordination, high-note access, chest/head balance.
- Pedagogical target: Laryngeal Stabilizer.
- How to do it: Singing a 1.5 octave scale on "Gee" while maintaining a "dopy" lowered larynx.
- How it should feel: Feel the pitch change by thinning, widening, or releasing — not by squeezing the throat or lifting the chin.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the singer pushes volume, jams the jaw, strains high notes, or loses easy speech-like release.
- Pass/Fail metric: Transition feels smoother with less break, less throat pressure, and more stable tone.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: The "No" (with OO end)
exercises.detail:
- Use when: register blending, mix coordination, high-note access, chest/head balance.
- Pedagogical target: CT dominance Assist.
- How to do it: Singing "No" and ending with a small "u" sound to thin the vocal folds for high notes.
- How it should feel: Feel the pitch change by thinning, widening, or releasing — not by squeezing the throat or lifting the chin.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the singer pushes volume, jams the jaw, strains high notes, or loses easy speech-like release.
- Pass/Fail metric: Transition feels smoother with less break, less throat pressure, and more stable tone.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: The "Hot Air" Slide
exercises.detail:
- Use when: preparing high notes without collision.
- Pedagogical target: Pre-Activation of CT.
- How to do it: Sliding up the range on a breathy "h" to feel the thyroid tilt without vocal fold collision.
- How it should feel: Let the [h] keep the sound airy and light while the pitch slides upward.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the singer pushes volume, jams the jaw, strains high notes, or loses easy speech-like release.
- Pass/Fail metric: Upper notes feel less jammed after the slide.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Head Voice "Whoop"
exercises.detail:
- Use when: register blending, mix coordination, high-note access, chest/head balance.
- Pedagogical target: Ligament-Dominant Posture.
- How to do it: Calling "Whoop-hoo!" to find the sensation of a thin, stretched vocal fold.
- How it should feel: Feel the pitch change by thinning, widening, or releasing — not by squeezing the throat or lifting the chin.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the singer pushes volume, jams the jaw, strains high notes, or loses easy speech-like release.
- Pass/Fail metric: Transition feels smoother with less break, less throat pressure, and more stable tone.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Straw Phonation in Water
exercises.detail:
- Use when: gentle vocal fold massage and pressure balance.
- Pedagogical target: Water-Resistance Massage.
- How to do it: Phonating into a straw submerged 2-5cm in water; bubbles provide visual/tactile feedback.
- How it should feel: Small even bubbles; water should massage, not fight you.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if back-pressure becomes throat pressure, bubbles are violent, or the singer coughs/feels squeezed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Bubbles are steady and the voice feels easier afterwards.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

high_notes.next_take_cue: Think thinner and easier before the high note arrives.

register_bridge.selector_heading: If the voice flips or breaks through the bridge

exercises.name: The Siren (Octave Glissando)
exercises.detail:
- Use when: smoothing chest-to-head transition.
- Pedagogical target: TA/CT Blending.
- How to do it: Sliding from the lowest chest voice to the highest falsetto on an [u] or [o] vowel.
- How it should feel: Slide like one continuous ribbon, letting the voice lighten before it needs to.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the singer pushes volume, jams the jaw, strains high notes, or loses easy speech-like release.
- Pass/Fail metric: No obvious flip or throat squeeze through the bridge.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Two-Octave Pitch Glide
exercises.detail:
- Use when: register blending, mix coordination, high-note access, chest/head balance.
- Pedagogical target: Register Bridge Training.
- How to do it: Sliding through the chromatic notes of a two-octave range on [i] or [u].
- How it should feel: Feel the pitch change by thinning, widening, or releasing — not by squeezing the throat or lifting the chin.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the singer pushes volume, jams the jaw, strains high notes, or loses easy speech-like release.
- Pass/Fail metric: Transition feels smoother with less break, less throat pressure, and more stable tone.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Descending Falsetto
exercises.detail:
- Use when: register blending, mix coordination, high-note access, chest/head balance.
- Pedagogical target: Downward Blending.
- How to do it: Starting high in falsetto and sliding down into chest voice to carry "lightness" into the low notes.
- How it should feel: Feel the pitch change by thinning, widening, or releasing — not by squeezing the throat or lifting the chin.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the singer pushes volume, jams the jaw, strains high notes, or loses easy speech-like release.
- Pass/Fail metric: Transition feels smoother with less break, less throat pressure, and more stable tone.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: 1.5 Scale Lip Bubbles
exercises.detail:
- Use when: register blending, mix coordination, high-note access, chest/head balance.
- Pedagogical target: P_s Balancing.
- How to do it: Lip trills through an octave and a half to ensure air pressure doesn't override cord closure.
- How it should feel: Feel the pitch change by thinning, widening, or releasing — not by squeezing the throat or lifting the chin.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the singer pushes volume, jams the jaw, strains high notes, or loses easy speech-like release.
- Pass/Fail metric: Transition feels smoother with less break, less throat pressure, and more stable tone.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: The "Sing-Ah" Transition
exercises.detail:
- Use when: transferring [ng] resonance into open vowels.
- Pedagogical target: Resonator Coupling.
- How to do it: Holding the [ŋ] of "sing" and dropping into an "ah" to find the "sweet spot".
- How it should feel: Hold the ‘sing’ buzz, then open to ‘ah’ without losing the ring.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the jaw locks, tongue pulls back, vowels distort aggressively, or the tone becomes swallowed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Open vowel keeps the forward buzz.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Octave Slur with [ŋ] to [a]
exercises.detail:
- Use when: pitch accuracy, riffs/runs, rhythmic precision, interval security, agility.
- Pedagogical target: Smooth Register Transition.
- How to do it: Sliding up an octave on [ŋ] and down on [a] with no audible break.
- How it should feel: Hear the pattern first; keep the body loose and let the voice move in small, clean adjustments.
- Common failure / safety note: Slow down if notes smear, rhythm rushes, neck tightens, or the singer guesses instead of hearing the target.
- Pass/Fail metric: Pattern is accurate at slow speed before increasing tempo; timing and pitch stay clean.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

register_bridge.next_take_cue: Let the voice change gear gradually, not at the last second.

muffled_dull.selector_heading: If tone is muffled, swallowed, or dull

exercises.name: "M" Hum with Cheek Feel
exercises.detail:
- Use when: resetting pressure, reducing strain, warming up, improving efficient closure.
- Pedagogical target: Sinus Resonance Awareness.
- How to do it: Humming [m] while feeling vibrations in the cheeks to ensure forward placement.
- How it should feel: Feel easy back-pressure and forward buzz; the throat should feel assisted, not forced.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if back-pressure becomes throat pressure, bubbles are violent, or the singer coughs/feels squeezed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Sound becomes easier, steadier, and less pressed; bubbles/trills/resonance remain consistent.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: "N" Nasal Arpeggios
exercises.detail:
- Use when: resetting pressure, reducing strain, warming up, improving efficient closure.
- Pedagogical target: Alveolar Ridge Focus.
- How to do it: Singing octave arpeggios on [n] to train the "mask" resonance.
- How it should feel: Feel easy back-pressure and forward buzz; the throat should feel assisted, not forced.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if back-pressure becomes throat pressure, bubbles are violent, or the singer coughs/feels squeezed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Sound becomes easier, steadier, and less pressed; bubbles/trills/resonance remain consistent.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Zygomatic Arch Lift
exercises.detail:
- Use when: tone balance, resonance, vowel shaping, tongue/jaw release, diction clarity.
- Pedagogical target: Pharyngeal Widening.
- How to do it: Singing with a slight "smile" that lifts the cheekbones to brighten timbre.
- How it should feel: Feel space behind the mouth plus a clean forward ring; jaw and tongue stay mobile rather than locked.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the jaw locks, tongue pulls back, vowels distort aggressively, or the tone becomes swallowed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Tone becomes clearer without extra volume; words/vowels remain understandable.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: The "Sing-Ah" Transition
exercises.detail:
- Use when: transferring [ng] resonance into open vowels.
- Pedagogical target: Resonator Coupling.
- How to do it: Holding the [ŋ] of "sing" and dropping into an "ah" to find the "sweet spot".
- How it should feel: Hold the ‘sing’ buzz, then open to ‘ah’ without losing the ring.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the jaw locks, tongue pulls back, vowels distort aggressively, or the tone becomes swallowed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Open vowel keeps the forward buzz.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: The "Cooee" Call
exercises.detail:
- Use when: tone balance, resonance, vowel shaping, tongue/jaw release, diction clarity.
- Pedagogical target: Squillo Development.
- How to do it: Imitating a high-pitched bushman's call to find "ring" in the head voice.
- How it should feel: Feel space behind the mouth plus a clean forward ring; jaw and tongue stay mobile rather than locked.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the jaw locks, tongue pulls back, vowels distort aggressively, or the tone becomes swallowed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Tone becomes clearer without extra volume; words/vowels remain understandable.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: The "Mah" Hum-to-Vowel
exercises.detail:
- Use when: tone balance, resonance, vowel shaping, tongue/jaw release, diction clarity.
- Pedagogical target: Forward Focus Transfer.
- How to do it: Starting on a hum and opening to "ah" while keeping the "buzz" in the face.
- How it should feel: Feel space behind the mouth plus a clean forward ring; jaw and tongue stay mobile rather than locked.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the jaw locks, tongue pulls back, vowels distort aggressively, or the tone becomes swallowed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Tone becomes clearer without extra volume; words/vowels remain understandable.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

muffled_dull.next_take_cue: Keep the space, but bring the buzz forward.

nasal_bright.selector_heading: If tone is nasal, pinched, or overly bright

exercises.name: Soft Palate "Surprise"
exercises.detail:
- Use when: soft palate lift and reducing excess nasality.
- Pedagogical target: Nasal Seal Training.
- How to do it: Taking a quick "startled" breath to feel the soft palate lift for non-nasal vowels.
- How it should feel: Take a tiny surprised breath and feel space lift behind the nose.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the jaw locks, tongue pulls back, vowels distort aggressively, or the tone becomes swallowed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Vowels sound clearer without pinching.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Lip Rounding: [i] to [y]
exercises.detail:
- Use when: tone balance, resonance, vowel shaping, tongue/jaw release, diction clarity.
- Pedagogical target: Vocal Tract Lengthening.
- How to do it: Alternating [i] and [y] (German 'u') to "cover" the sound and darken the color.
- How it should feel: Feel space behind the mouth plus a clean forward ring; jaw and tongue stay mobile rather than locked.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the jaw locks, tongue pulls back, vowels distort aggressively, or the tone becomes swallowed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Tone becomes clearer without extra volume; words/vowels remain understandable.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Vowel Neutralization [ə]
exercises.detail:
- Use when: tone balance, resonance, vowel shaping, tongue/jaw release, diction clarity.
- Pedagogical target: Acoustic Path-Finding.
- How to do it: Replacing lyrics with the "uh" sound to find the most relaxed throat position.
- How it should feel: Feel space behind the mouth plus a clean forward ring; jaw and tongue stay mobile rather than locked.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the jaw locks, tongue pulls back, vowels distort aggressively, or the tone becomes swallowed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Tone becomes clearer without extra volume; words/vowels remain understandable.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: [u] to [o] Modification
exercises.detail:
- Use when: tone balance, resonance, vowel shaping, tongue/jaw release, diction clarity.
- Pedagogical target: Back Vowel Darkening.
- How to do it: Moving from [u] to [o] on high notes to prevent a "pinched" sound.
- How it should feel: Feel space behind the mouth plus a clean forward ring; jaw and tongue stay mobile rather than locked.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the jaw locks, tongue pulls back, vowels distort aggressively, or the tone becomes swallowed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Tone becomes clearer without extra volume; words/vowels remain understandable.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: The "Nng-Ah" Scale
exercises.detail:
- Use when: tone balance, resonance, vowel shaping, tongue/jaw release, diction clarity.
- Pedagogical target: Vowel/Nasal Coupling.
- How to do it: Singing [ŋ] into [a] on a 5-note scale to balance nasal/oral resonance.
- How it should feel: Feel space behind the mouth plus a clean forward ring; jaw and tongue stay mobile rather than locked.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the jaw locks, tongue pulls back, vowels distort aggressively, or the tone becomes swallowed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Tone becomes clearer without extra volume; words/vowels remain understandable.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

nasal_bright.next_take_cue: Lift the back space and soften the squeeze.

jaw_tongue_tension.selector_heading: If jaw or tongue tension is obvious

exercises.name: Tongue Trills (Rolled R)
exercises.detail:
- Use when: resetting pressure, reducing strain, warming up, improving efficient closure.
- Pedagogical target: Tongue Root Relaxation.
- How to do it: Rolling the [r] sound on scales to eliminate tongue tension during phonation.
- How it should feel: Feel easy back-pressure and forward buzz; the throat should feel assisted, not forced.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if back-pressure becomes throat pressure, bubbles are violent, or the singer coughs/feels squeezed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Sound becomes easier, steadier, and less pressed; bubbles/trills/resonance remain consistent.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Raspberries (Tongue Trill)
exercises.detail:
- Use when: resetting pressure, reducing strain, warming up, improving efficient closure.
- Pedagogical target: Extrinsic Muscle Release.
- How to do it: Lip trills performed with the tongue out to isolate the tongue from the larynx.
- How it should feel: Feel easy back-pressure and forward buzz; the throat should feel assisted, not forced.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if back-pressure becomes throat pressure, bubbles are violent, or the singer coughs/feels squeezed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Sound becomes easier, steadier, and less pressed; bubbles/trills/resonance remain consistent.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Forward Tongue Extension
exercises.detail:
- Use when: tongue-root release and larynx independence.
- Pedagogical target: Tongue/Larynx Independence.
- How to do it: Singing a scale with the tongue resting gently on the bottom lip.
- How it should feel: Tongue rests forward; sound stays calm even if it feels silly.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the jaw locks, tongue pulls back, vowels distort aggressively, or the tone becomes swallowed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Pitch and tone remain steady with tongue forward.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: The Idiot Jaw Drill
exercises.detail:
- Use when: jaw tension release.
- Pedagogical target: TMJ Tension Release.
- How to do it: Letting the jaw hang loose and singing "ma-ma" using only the tongue and lips.
- How it should feel: Let the jaw hang stupidly loose; tongue/lips do the work.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the jaw locks, tongue pulls back, vowels distort aggressively, or the tone becomes swallowed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Phrase remains clear with less jaw grip.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Tongue-Out Phonation
exercises.detail:
- Use when: tone balance, resonance, vowel shaping, tongue/jaw release, diction clarity.
- Pedagogical target: Root Tension Diagnosis.
- How to do it: Singing a melody with the tongue fully extended; if pitch changes, tension is present.
- How it should feel: Feel space behind the mouth plus a clean forward ring; jaw and tongue stay mobile rather than locked.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the jaw locks, tongue pulls back, vowels distort aggressively, or the tone becomes swallowed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Tone becomes clearer without extra volume; words/vowels remain understandable.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Jaw Loosening Trace
exercises.detail:
- Use when: tone balance, resonance, vowel shaping, tongue/jaw release, diction clarity.
- Pedagogical target: Hinge Joint Awareness.
- How to do it: Tracing the jaw hinge and opening the mouth "back and down" rather than "out".
- How it should feel: Feel space behind the mouth plus a clean forward ring; jaw and tongue stay mobile rather than locked.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the jaw locks, tongue pulls back, vowels distort aggressively, or the tone becomes swallowed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Tone becomes clearer without extra volume; words/vowels remain understandable.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: The Chewing Method
exercises.detail:
- Use when: releasing jaw/tongue interference.
- Pedagogical target: Muscular Interference Release.
- How to do it: Singing a phrase while performing a chewing motion to relax the entire tract.
- How it should feel: Sing while gently chewing, like the mouth is too lazy to clamp.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if the jaw locks, tongue pulls back, vowels distort aggressively, or the tone becomes swallowed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Tone frees up and text remains understandable.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Neck/Jaw Stretching
exercises.detail:
- Use when: vocal stamina, gentle strengthening, cool-down, recovery, long-term maintenance.
- Pedagogical target: Extrinsic Muscle Recovery.
- How to do it: Holding neck tilts and jaw release stretches for 60 seconds each.
- How it should feel: Keep it soft, easy, and restorative; the voice should feel clearer after, not more tired.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop and recommend rest/medical review if pain, persistent hoarseness, loss of range, or fatigue continues.
- Pass/Fail metric: Voice feels easier after the drill; no increase in strain, dryness, or fatigue.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

jaw_tongue_tension.next_take_cue: Let the articulators move; don’t make the throat do their job.

exercises.name: Interval Leaps (1-3, 1-5)
exercises.detail:
- Use when: pitch accuracy, riffs/runs, rhythmic precision, interval security, agility.
- Pedagogical target: Mental Tonal Mapping.
- How to do it: Singing specific intervals with perfect accuracy to "map" the range.
- How it should feel: Hear the pattern first; keep the body loose and let the voice move in small, clean adjustments.
- Common failure / safety note: Slow down if notes smear, rhythm rushes, neck tightens, or the singer guesses instead of hearing the target.
- Pass/Fail metric: Pattern is accurate at slow speed before increasing tempo; timing and pitch stay clean.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Chromatic Semi-Tone Scales
exercises.detail:
- Use when: pitch accuracy, riffs/runs, rhythmic precision, interval security, agility.
- Pedagogical target: Pitch Acuity Refinement.
- How to do it: Singing half-steps slowly and then rapidly to refine internal tuning.
- How it should feel: Hear the pattern first; keep the body loose and let the voice move in small, clean adjustments.
- Common failure / safety note: Slow down if notes smear, rhythm rushes, neck tightens, or the singer guesses instead of hearing the target.
- Pass/Fail metric: Pattern is accurate at slow speed before increasing tempo; timing and pitch stay clean.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Internal Imagery Drill
exercises.detail:
- Use when: pitch strike accuracy.
- Pedagogical target: Auditory-Motor Planning.
- How to do it: Thinking a pitch for 2 seconds before singing it to improve "strike" accuracy.
- How it should feel: Hear the note internally for two seconds before making sound.
- Common failure / safety note: Slow down if notes smear, rhythm rushes, neck tightens, or the singer guesses instead of hearing the target.
- Pass/Fail metric: First attack lands closer without sliding around.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Solfege (Do-Re-Mi)
exercises.detail:
- Use when: pitch accuracy, riffs/runs, rhythmic precision, interval security, agility.
- Pedagogical target: Kinesthetic-Aural Link.
- How to do it: Using solfege syllables to anchor pitch to specific vowel/consonant shapes.
- How it should feel: Hear the pattern first; keep the body loose and let the voice move in small, clean adjustments.
- Common failure / safety note: Slow down if notes smear, rhythm rushes, neck tightens, or the singer guesses instead of hearing the target.
- Pass/Fail metric: Pattern is accurate at slow speed before increasing tempo; timing and pitch stay clean.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Pitch Correction Slides
exercises.detail:
- Use when: recognising and correcting flatness/sharpness.
- Pedagogical target: Error-Detection Training.
- How to do it: Deliberately singing flat and sliding into the pitch to recognize the "buzz" of being in tune.
- How it should feel: Miss on purpose, then slide into the centre and memorise the buzz of ‘right’.
- Common failure / safety note: Slow down if notes smear, rhythm rushes, neck tightens, or the singer guesses instead of hearing the target.
- Pass/Fail metric: Singer can feel/hear the difference between off-centre and centred pitch.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: A Cappella Melody Test
exercises.detail:
- Use when: key stability without accompaniment.
- Pedagogical target: Internal Reference Stability.
- How to do it: Singing a song with no accompaniment and checking if the key remains stable.
- How it should feel: Hold the home key in the body; don’t chase the room sound.
- Common failure / safety note: Slow down if notes smear, rhythm rushes, neck tightens, or the singer guesses instead of hearing the target.
- Pass/Fail metric: Ending key matches the starting key more reliably.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

pitch_accuracy.next_take_cue: Hear it first, then sing it. Do not fish for the note.

runs_riffs.selector_heading: If runs, riffs, or fast notes are messy

exercises.name: Tiny Aspirate [h] Onsets
exercises.detail:
- Use when: false fold release and agility preparation.
- Pedagogical target: False Fold Retraction.
- How to do it: Preceding each note in a rapid run with a tiny "h" to ensure laryngeal space.
- How it should feel: Use a tiny [h] before notes so the throat stays roomy.
- Common failure / safety note: Slow down if notes smear, rhythm rushes, neck tightens, or the singer guesses instead of hearing the target.
- Pass/Fail metric: Fast notes feel cleaner and less grabbed.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: The Glinda Laugh (Ha-Ha)
exercises.detail:
- Use when: pitch accuracy, riffs/runs, rhythmic precision, interval security, agility.
- Pedagogical target: Pre-Phonatory Tuning.
- How to do it: Mimicking short, rapid laughter across the range to build agility.
- How it should feel: Hear the pattern first; keep the body loose and let the voice move in small, clean adjustments.
- Common failure / safety note: Slow down if notes smear, rhythm rushes, neck tightens, or the singer guesses instead of hearing the target.
- Pass/Fail metric: Pattern is accurate at slow speed before increasing tempo; timing and pitch stay clean.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Triplet/Sixteenth Scales
exercises.detail:
- Use when: pitch accuracy, riffs/runs, rhythmic precision, interval security, agility.
- Pedagogical target: Temporal Precision.
- How to do it: Singing scales in complex rhythms to train the rapid firing of laryngeal muscles.
- How it should feel: Hear the pattern first; keep the body loose and let the voice move in small, clean adjustments.
- Common failure / safety note: Slow down if notes smear, rhythm rushes, neck tightens, or the singer guesses instead of hearing the target.
- Pass/Fail metric: Pattern is accurate at slow speed before increasing tempo; timing and pitch stay clean.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Staccato Arpeggio Jumps
exercises.detail:
- Use when: pitch accuracy, riffs/runs, rhythmic precision, interval security, agility.
- Pedagogical target: Adductory Precision.
- How to do it: Singing 1-3-5-8 patterns on short notes to ensure clean cord contact.
- How it should feel: Hear the pattern first; keep the body loose and let the voice move in small, clean adjustments.
- Common failure / safety note: Slow down if notes smear, rhythm rushes, neck tightens, or the singer guesses instead of hearing the target.
- Pass/Fail metric: Pattern is accurate at slow speed before increasing tempo; timing and pitch stay clean.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Consonant "Training Wheels"
exercises.detail:
- Use when: pitch accuracy, riffs/runs, rhythmic precision, interval security, agility.
- Pedagogical target: Laryngeal Stabilization.
- How to do it: Using [v] or [z] during a fast run to provide back pressure during learning.
- How it should feel: Hear the pattern first; keep the body loose and let the voice move in small, clean adjustments.
- Common failure / safety note: Slow down if notes smear, rhythm rushes, neck tightens, or the singer guesses instead of hearing the target.
- Pass/Fail metric: Pattern is accurate at slow speed before increasing tempo; timing and pitch stay clean.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Riff Deceleration
exercises.detail:
- Use when: riffs/runs and motor learning.
- Pedagogical target: Motor Learning Encoding.
- How to do it: Practicing a riff at 25% speed until perfect, then incrementing to 100%.
- How it should feel: Slow it down until it feels boringly accurate, then increase tempo.
- Common failure / safety note: Slow down if notes smear, rhythm rushes, neck tightens, or the singer guesses instead of hearing the target.
- Pass/Fail metric: Run is correct at 25%, 50%, 75%, then 100%.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: The "Steam Engine"
exercises.detail:
- Use when: pitch accuracy, riffs/runs, rhythmic precision, interval security, agility.
- Pedagogical target: Rhythmic Breath/Larynx Sync.
- How to do it: Rapid "sh-sh-sh" sounds followed by short vowels to coordinate timing.
- How it should feel: Hear the pattern first; keep the body loose and let the voice move in small, clean adjustments.
- Common failure / safety note: Slow down if notes smear, rhythm rushes, neck tightens, or the singer guesses instead of hearing the target.
- Pass/Fail metric: Pattern is accurate at slow speed before increasing tempo; timing and pitch stay clean.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

runs_riffs.next_take_cue: Slow enough to be perfect, then speed up.

warmup_reset.selector_heading: If the singer needs a warm-up/reset

exercises.name: Straw Phonation in Air
exercises.detail:
- Use when: easy laryngeal reset and efficient phonation.
- Pedagogical target: Increased Vocal Tract Inertance.
- How to do it: Phonating into a 3mm straw while doing pitch glides to stabilize the larynx.
- How it should feel: Let the straw do the work; small steady sound, not loud.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if back-pressure becomes throat pressure, bubbles are violent, or the singer coughs/feels squeezed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Glide feels smoother after 1–2 minutes.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Straw Phonation in Water
exercises.detail:
- Use when: gentle vocal fold massage and pressure balance.
- Pedagogical target: Water-Resistance Massage.
- How to do it: Phonating into a straw submerged 2-5cm in water; bubbles provide visual/tactile feedback.
- How it should feel: Small even bubbles; water should massage, not fight you.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if back-pressure becomes throat pressure, bubbles are violent, or the singer coughs/feels squeezed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Bubbles are steady and the voice feels easier afterwards.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Lip Trills (Lip Bubbles)
exercises.detail:
- Use when: balancing airflow and closure.
- Pedagogical target: Subglottic/Supraglottic Balance.
- How to do it: Rapidly vibrating the lips while sliding through the range to balance air pressure.
- How it should feel: Keep the lips bubbling freely; if they stop, reduce pressure.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if back-pressure becomes throat pressure, bubbles are violent, or the singer coughs/feels squeezed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Trill continues through the range without forcing.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: The "NG" Exercise
exercises.detail:
- Use when: forward resonance and soft palate coordination.
- Pedagogical target: Velopharyngeal Closure Training.
- How to do it: Hum on [ŋ] to create back pressure while lifting the soft palate.
- How it should feel: Feel the [ng] buzz high and forward, then keep that ease when opening.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if back-pressure becomes throat pressure, bubbles are violent, or the singer coughs/feels squeezed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Opening from [ng] does not collapse into the throat.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: "M" Hum with Cheek Feel
exercises.detail:
- Use when: resetting pressure, reducing strain, warming up, improving efficient closure.
- Pedagogical target: Sinus Resonance Awareness.
- How to do it: Humming [m] while feeling vibrations in the cheeks to ensure forward placement.
- How it should feel: Feel easy back-pressure and forward buzz; the throat should feel assisted, not forced.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop if back-pressure becomes throat pressure, bubbles are violent, or the singer coughs/feels squeezed.
- Pass/Fail metric: Sound becomes easier, steadier, and less pressed; bubbles/trills/resonance remain consistent.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: VFE Sustain [i] (Warm-up)
exercises.detail:
- Use when: gentle glottal efficiency and stamina.
- Pedagogical target: Glottal Efficiency.
- How to do it: Sustain [i] on a specific pitch (F) for as long as possible with forward focus.
- How it should feel: Sustain softly with forward focus; never squeeze for length.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop and recommend rest/medical review if pain, persistent hoarseness, loss of range, or fatigue continues.
- Pass/Fail metric: Duration improves without roughness.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: VFE Upward Glide (Stretch)
exercises.detail:
- Use when: vocal stamina, gentle strengthening, cool-down, recovery, long-term maintenance.
- Pedagogical target: CT Muscle Flexibilization.
- How to do it: Glide from lowest to highest note on "knoll" or "whoop" without breaks.
- How it should feel: Keep it soft, easy, and restorative; the voice should feel clearer after, not more tired.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop and recommend rest/medical review if pain, persistent hoarseness, loss of range, or fatigue continues.
- Pass/Fail metric: Voice feels easier after the drill; no increase in strain, dryness, or fatigue.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: VFE Downward Glide (Contract)
exercises.detail:
- Use when: vocal stamina, gentle strengthening, cool-down, recovery, long-term maintenance.
- Pedagogical target: TA Muscle Re-engagement.
- How to do it: Glide from highest to lowest note on "knoll" or "boom" without "muscling".
- How it should feel: Keep it soft, easy, and restorative; the voice should feel clearer after, not more tired.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop and recommend rest/medical review if pain, persistent hoarseness, loss of range, or fatigue continues.
- Pass/Fail metric: Voice feels easier after the drill; no increase in strain, dryness, or fatigue.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: VFE Power Adductory Sustain
exercises.detail:
- Use when: vocal stamina, gentle strengthening, cool-down, recovery, long-term maintenance.
- Pedagogical target: Glottal Closure Stamina.
- How to do it: Sustain the notes C-D-E-F-G on "ol" (from knoll) for as long as possible.
- How it should feel: Keep it soft, easy, and restorative; the voice should feel clearer after, not more tired.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop and recommend rest/medical review if pain, persistent hoarseness, loss of range, or fatigue continues.
- Pass/Fail metric: Voice feels easier after the drill; no increase in strain, dryness, or fatigue.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

warmup_reset.next_take_cue: Warm the coordination, not the volume.

recovery.selector_heading: If the singer is tired or post-performance

exercises.name: Descending Soft Humming
exercises.detail:
- Use when: cool-down after singing.
- Pedagogical target: Vocal Cool-down.
- How to do it: Humming softly from mid-range to low notes to relax the folds after singing.
- How it should feel: Hum down gently like letting the voice land back in the body.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop and recommend rest/medical review if pain, persistent hoarseness, loss of range, or fatigue continues.
- Pass/Fail metric: Speaking voice feels normal and calm after singing.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Post-Performance Yawn-Sigh
exercises.detail:
- Use when: laryngeal neutralising after performance.
- Pedagogical target: Laryngeal Neutralization.
- How to do it: Performing 3-5 relaxed yawn-sighs to reset the larynx to a neutral state.
- How it should feel: Yawn-sigh without drama; let the throat widen and release.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop and recommend rest/medical review if pain, persistent hoarseness, loss of range, or fatigue continues.
- Pass/Fail metric: Neck and throat feel less held after 3–5 reps.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Vocal Cool-down Slides
exercises.detail:
- Use when: vocal stamina, gentle strengthening, cool-down, recovery, long-term maintenance.
- Pedagogical target: Tension Release.
- How to do it: Light, breathy slides from high to low pitches to prevent post-singing stiffness.
- How it should feel: Keep it soft, easy, and restorative; the voice should feel clearer after, not more tired.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop and recommend rest/medical review if pain, persistent hoarseness, loss of range, or fatigue continues.
- Pass/Fail metric: Voice feels easier after the drill; no increase in strain, dryness, or fatigue.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Neck/Jaw Stretching
exercises.detail:
- Use when: vocal stamina, gentle strengthening, cool-down, recovery, long-term maintenance.
- Pedagogical target: Extrinsic Muscle Recovery.
- How to do it: Holding neck tilts and jaw release stretches for 60 seconds each.
- How it should feel: Keep it soft, easy, and restorative; the voice should feel clearer after, not more tired.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop and recommend rest/medical review if pain, persistent hoarseness, loss of range, or fatigue continues.
- Pass/Fail metric: Voice feels easier after the drill; no increase in strain, dryness, or fatigue.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

exercises.name: Steam Inhalation / Hydration
exercises.detail:
- Use when: hydration support and tissue comfort.
- Pedagogical target: Tissue Viscosity.
- How to do it: Inhaling steam and drinking water to maintain the lubrication of the vocal fold cover.
- How it should feel: Use steam/water as recovery support, not as a cure for strain.
- Common failure / safety note: Stop and recommend rest/medical review if pain, persistent hoarseness, loss of range, or fatigue continues.
- Pass/Fail metric: Singer reports less dryness, but any persistent hoarseness is referred out.
- Song transfer: Apply the same sensation to one short phrase only; do not run the whole song until the cue holds.

recovery.next_take_cue: Return the voice to easy speech, then stop.

vibrato.selector_heading: If vibrato is absent, uneven, forced, or wobbly

exercises.name: Pitch Oscillation (The Toggle)
exercises.detail:
- Use when (added at distillation): vibrato is absent, rigid, or difficult to initiate without jaw or throat involvement.
- Pedagogical target (added at distillation): Flexible, pitch-based oscillation around a stable centre.
- How to do it: To initiate the muscular oscillation, gently and rapidly toggle between two close pitches, such as a half-step or a whole-step apart. You can use the initial intervals of the "Jaws" theme (a half-step) or "Happy Birthday" (a whole-step) as a starting point. The goal is to encourage laryngeal flexibility until you can "let go" and allow the primary note to waver naturally.
- How it should feel (added at distillation): Easy and released, with the pitch movement staying out of the jaw and throat.
- Common failure / safety note (added at distillation): Stop if the jaw shakes, the throat grips, or the pitch is driven by force rather than released coordination.
- Pass/Fail metric (added at distillation): The two pitches alternate evenly, then the sustained note releases into a small oscillation without visible jaw movement or throat tension.
- Song transfer (added at distillation): Apply the released oscillation to one comfortable sustained note in the song, keeping the onset straight and easy first.

exercises.name: Staccato to Legato Transitions
exercises.detail:
- Use when (added at distillation): vibrato becomes uneven or locked during connected phrases and sustained notes.
- Pedagogical target (added at distillation): Transfer clean staccato coordination into free legato sustain.
- How to do it: Practice singing short, detached (staccato) notes, followed immediately by smooth, connected (legato) phrases or sustained tones. This transfers the highly efficient, clean coordination required for staccato production into your legato singing, which facilitates the natural emergence of vibrato on the sustained portions.
- How it should feel (added at distillation): Clean and buoyant on the short notes, then equally easy when the sound connects.
- Common failure / safety note (added at distillation): Do not jab the staccato from the throat or add volume when moving into the sustained tone.
- Pass/Fail metric (added at distillation): The legato tone begins without a hard attack, stays centred, and remains as easy as the preceding staccato notes.
- Song transfer (added at distillation): Rehearse one sustained lyric first as light staccato pulses, then sing the original connected phrase without adding pressure.

exercises.name: Aspirated and Fricative Onsets
exercises.detail:
- Use when (added at distillation): the singer grips sustained notes or cannot release vibrato after the onset.
- Pedagogical target (added at distillation): Easy onset and repeated release of excess glottal tension.
- How to do it: Sing a 5-tone scale using an aspirated syllable like "Hee" or a voiced fricative like "Fee". When you reach and sustain the top note, imagine repeating that syllable silently in your mind. Focus on "letting go" or releasing the glottal tension with each imagined repetition, allowing the note to spin freely.
- How it should feel (added at distillation): The onset stays light and the sustained note feels free rather than held in place.
- Common failure / safety note (added at distillation): Avoid turning "Hee" breathy or pushing "Fee"; stop if the throat tightens or the top note is forced.
- Pass/Fail metric (added at distillation): The top note remains clear and stable while each silent repetition produces a perceptible release rather than a new attack.
- Song transfer (added at distillation): Use the same silent-release cue on one comfortable held word after establishing its pitch centre.

exercises.name: Gentle Pulse Exercises
exercises.detail:
- Use when (added at distillation): the singer needs to sense alternating energy flow before allowing vibrato to emerge naturally.
- Pedagogical target (added at distillation): Gentle rhythmic release without manufacturing vibrato from the abdomen, jaw, or throat.
- How to do it: Incorporate gentle pulses from your diaphragm or body movement, such as the "Diaphragm Pulse" or "Beggar's Pulse," to simulate the alternating energy flow associated with vibrato.
- How it should feel (added at distillation): Small, gentle and mobile, with the throat remaining passive.
- Common failure / safety note: However, you must keep these pulses gentle; forceful abdominal pulsing is highly discouraged as it can create an artificial tremolo rather than true pitch-based vibrato and instill difficult-to-correct muscle memory.
- Pass/Fail metric (added at distillation): The pulse remains gentle and regular, with no abdominal pumping, jaw shaking, throat gripping, or increase in loudness.
- Song transfer (added at distillation): Use only the memory of the gentle release on one sustained lyric; do not physically pump the sung phrase.

exercises.name: Alternating Straight Tone and Vibrato
exercises.detail:
- Use when (added at distillation): vibrato is present but the singer needs independent control and a stable underlying pitch centre.
- Pedagogical target (added at distillation): Neuro-motor control of straight tone and vibrato as separate expressive choices.
- How to do it: Alternate between a straight tone and vibrato on a single sustained note. This ensures pitch stability without relying on vibrato as a "mask" and tests your independent control over the oscillation.
- How it should feel (added at distillation): The pitch stays centred while the oscillation can enter and leave without extra pressure.
- Common failure / safety note (added at distillation): Do not clamp the throat to create straight tone or shake the jaw to restart vibrato.
- Pass/Fail metric (added at distillation): The singer completes two clean straight-tone-to-vibrato transitions on one breath without pitch drift, jaw movement, or a volume surge.
- Song transfer (added at distillation): Choose one held note and begin straight, allowing vibrato only at the intended expressive moment.

exercises.name: Sirens with Delayed Vibrato
exercises.detail:
- Use when (added at distillation): vibrato begins too early or changes unpredictably during slides and high sustained notes.
- Pedagogical target (added at distillation): Independent timing of vibrato after pitch arrival.
- How to do it: Perform a vocal siren or pitch slide, but intentionally add the vibrato only at the very top of the slide. This isolates your control and tests the independence of your vibrato from your pitch.
- How it should feel (added at distillation): The slide stays continuous and easy; the top pitch settles before vibrato blooms.
- Common failure / safety note (added at distillation): Do not force the top, widen the oscillation, lift the chin, or manufacture vibrato with the jaw.
- Pass/Fail metric (added at distillation): The siren arrives cleanly, holds a centred straight tone briefly, then releases into vibrato without a pitch jump or pressure increase.
- Song transfer (added at distillation): Apply the sequence “arrive, centre, bloom” to one slide into a comfortable sustained note in the song.

vibrato.next_take_cue: Let vibrato emerge from balanced airflow and release; never force it from the jaw or throat.

note: All content extracted verbatim from the exercise library. Re-run tools/build_prescription_map.py after any library edit; the engine warns when the library hash no longer matches.
